expanded_description: Combining notes into sentences is the core mechanical skill tested by Rhetorical Synthesis questions on the SAT. Students are presented with a set of bullet-point notes containing discrete facts and must evaluate which of several candidate sentences most effectively synthesizes those facts into a single, coherent, and rhetorically purposeful whole. This skill goes beyond simple summarization: it requires strategic decisions about subordination and coordination, about logical connectors and transitions, about what to foreground and what to background, and about how to arrange information so that the sentence flows naturally while achieving the specified rhetorical goal. Mastering this skill requires students to understand how sentence structure creates meaning and emphasis, and to recognize the difference between a sentence that merely lists facts and one that weaves them into a unified, purposeful statement.

core_concept.principle:
Combining notes into a single coherent sentence is an act of rhetorical design. The sentence is not a container into which facts are poured; it is a structure in which the arrangement of information creates relationships, establishes emphasis, and serves a purpose. The same set of bullet points can be combined into many different grammatically correct sentences, but only some of those sentences will achieve a given rhetorical goal effectively. The skilled writer and the skilled SAT test-taker both understand that sentence structure is a tool for meaning-making, not just a grammatical requirement.

The process of evaluating a synthesized sentence involves attention to four structural dimensions:

(1) Subordination and Coordination: Which pieces of information are placed in main clauses (given the greatest structural emphasis) and which are placed in subordinate clauses, phrases, or modifiers (given lesser emphasis)? The main clause carries the sentence's central assertion; subordinate elements provide supporting, contextual, or qualifying information. The rhetorical goal determines what belongs in the main clause and what should be subordinated.

(2) Logical Connectors and Transitions: What words or phrases signal the logical relationships among the pieces of information? Connectors like 'because,' 'although,' 'whereas,' 'while,' 'after,' 'as a result,' and 'in addition' explicitly state how the ideas relate to each other. The rhetorical goal determines which logical relationship should be the primary one expressed in the sentence.

(3) Information Sequencing: In what order are the pieces of information presented? The beginning and end of a sentence are positions of natural emphasis. Information placed at the beginning sets the frame or context; information placed at the end receives the stress of closure. The rhetorical goal determines what information belongs in these positions of emphasis.

(4) Stylistic Economy: How efficiently does the sentence integrate the information? Good synthesis avoids redundancy, eliminates wordiness, and creates smooth connections between ideas. The sentence should read as a single, flowing statement, not as a list of facts separated by commas or 'and.'
core_concept.explanation:
Consider the bullet points: 'The Amazon rainforest spans nine South American countries. It produces approximately 20% of the world's oxygen. Deforestation rates have increased by 22% in the past year.' These facts can be combined into a single sentence in many ways. 'The Amazon rainforest, which spans nine South American countries and produces approximately 20% of the world's oxygen, has seen deforestation rates increase by 22% in the past year.' This sentence places the deforestation increase in the main clause (emphasizing the problem), while subordinating the geographic scope and oxygen production as background context. 'Although the Amazon rainforest spans nine South American countries and produces approximately 20% of the world's oxygen, deforestation rates have increased by 22% in the past year.' The 'although' clause sets up a contrast between the rainforest's importance and the threat it faces—a different rhetorical move. 'The Amazon rainforest produces approximately 20% of the world's oxygen and spans nine South American countries, yet deforestation rates have increased by 22% in the past year.' The 'yet' creates a different logical relationship again. Each sentence is factually accurate, but each has a different rhetorical emphasis created by its structure. The SAT tests whether the student can identify the structure that matches the specified goal.

On the SAT, incorrect answer choices for note-combining questions typically fail in identifiable ways. Some choices simply list the facts without establishing clear logical relationships among them—a 'laundry list' sentence. Some choices place the wrong information in the main clause, emphasizing a detail that is tangential to the rhetorical goal. Some choices use logical connectors that misrepresent the relationships among the facts. Some choices are grammatically correct but stylistically awkward, with choppy rhythms or redundant phrasing. The correct choice is the one whose structural design—subordination, connectors, sequencing, economy—best serves the stated purpose.

image.alt: A diagram showing bullet-point notes on the left being transformed through four structural operations—Subordination/Coordination, Logical Connectors, Sequencing, Economy—into a single coherent sentence on the right. Each operation is represented by a gear or lever, suggesting that the writer manipulates these dimensions to achieve the desired effect.
image.caption: Combining notes into a sentence involves four structural operations that determine how information is organized, connected, and emphasized

sections.section_title: Foundation: The Structural Tools of Synthesis
sections.introduction: Before students can evaluate synthesized sentences on the SAT, they must understand the grammatical and rhetorical tools that writers use to combine multiple pieces of information into a single, coherent statement. This section defines the major structural tools—subordination, coordination, logical connectors, sequencing, and economy—and provides examples of how each tool affects meaning and emphasis.

key_concept.definition:
The structural tools of synthesis are the grammatical and rhetorical devices that allow a writer to integrate multiple facts into a single sentence with clear logical relationships and appropriate emphasis. These tools are not merely decorative; they are the mechanisms through which a writer communicates what is most important, what is supporting context, what contrasts with what, and what leads to or follows from what.

The major structural tools are:

Main Clause Placement (Subordination and Coordination): The main clause of a sentence carries its central assertion—the idea the sentence exists to communicate. Information placed in subordinate clauses (introduced by words like 'although,' 'because,' 'while,' 'when,' 'which') or in phrases (participial phrases, appositives, prepositional phrases) is backgrounded, supporting the main assertion. A writer can change the rhetorical effect of a sentence dramatically by changing which information occupies the main clause and which is subordinated.

Logical Connectors: Conjunctions and transitional words explicitly state the logical relationship between pieces of information. 'Because' signals causation; 'although' signals concession; 'whereas' signals contrast; 'after' signals temporal sequence; 'in addition' signals accumulation. The choice of connector determines how the reader understands the relationship among the facts.

Information Sequence: The order in which information appears affects emphasis and coherence. The opening of a sentence sets the frame or topic; the ending receives natural stress. Moving information from one position to another within the sentence changes its rhetorical weight.

Stylistic Economy: Effective synthesis eliminates redundancy, avoids unnecessary words, and creates smooth transitions between ideas. A sentence that repeats the same information in different words, or that strings facts together with only 'and' as a connector, is stylistically weak. A sentence that integrates facts efficiently, using precise vocabulary and varied sentence structures, is stylistically strong.
key_concept.context: SAT Rhetorical Synthesis questions require the student to apply these structural tools analytically, evaluating which candidate sentence uses them most effectively to achieve the stated rhetorical goal. The student does not need to name the tools but must recognize their effects.

categories.description: How the choice of what goes in the main clause shapes the rhetorical effect of a sentence.

examples.process: The Main Clause as the Central Assertion
examples.explanation:
Every sentence has a main clause—the subject and verb that form the grammatical core and carry the primary assertion. Information in the main clause is structurally emphasized; it is what the sentence is fundamentally about. Information in subordinate clauses and phrases is structurally backgrounded; it provides context, qualification, or support. The rhetorical goal determines what information deserves the main clause emphasis.

Example: Given the facts 'The hurricane made landfall on Tuesday' and 'The hurricane caused widespread flooding,' two different syntheses are possible depending on the goal. If the goal is to describe the hurricane's effects: 'The hurricane, which made landfall on Tuesday, caused widespread flooding.' The main clause is about the flooding—the effect is emphasized. If the goal is to establish a timeline of events: 'The hurricane caused widespread flooding after it made landfall on Tuesday' or 'When the hurricane made landfall on Tuesday, it caused widespread flooding.' In both, the sequencing is foregrounded. The choice of main clause depends on the rhetorical purpose.

An incorrect SAT choice will often place tangential information in the main clause while subordinating the information most relevant to the goal, creating a mismatch between structure and purpose.

categories.description: How conjunctions and transitions establish the logical architecture of a sentence.

examples.explanation:
Logical connectors are not merely glue; they are meaning-makers. They tell the reader how to understand the relationship between the ideas being combined. Using the wrong connector can misrepresent the relationship or create an incoherent statement. Using the right connector aligns the sentence's logic with the rhetorical goal.

Contrast connectors: 'whereas,' 'while,' 'although,' 'however,' 'in contrast,' 'unlike,' 'on the other hand.' These signal difference or opposition.
Similarity connectors: 'similarly,' 'likewise,' 'both,' 'as well as,' 'in the same way.' These signal commonality.
Causal connectors: 'because,' 'since,' 'as a result,' 'due to,' 'consequently,' 'therefore,' 'thus.' These signal cause-and-effect.
Sequential connectors: 'after,' 'before,' 'subsequently,' 'then,' 'eventually,' 'following,' 'once.' These signal temporal or logical order.
Additive connectors: 'and,' 'also,' 'in addition,' 'furthermore,' 'moreover.' These signal accumulation without specifying relationship.
Concessive connectors: 'although,' 'though,' 'even though,' 'despite,' 'while it is true that.' These signal an acknowledgment followed by a counterpoint.

An incorrect SAT choice may use a connector that implies the wrong relationship—for example, using 'because' when the notes only establish sequence, not causation, or using 'similarly' when the notes describe contrasting facts.

worked_examples.title: Evaluating Sentence Structures for Syntheses
worked_examples.problem:
Read the following prompt and notes, then evaluate the four candidate sentences to determine which best synthesizes the notes to achieve the stated goal. Prompt: A student is writing an article about local environmental initiatives. The student wants to emphasize what the two initiatives have in common. Notes:
- Initiative 1: 'Green Roofs Program,' started in 2020, provides grants for rooftop gardens on commercial buildings, has covered 120 buildings so far.
- Initiative 2: 'Urban Forest Project,' started in 2021, plants trees along city streets, has planted 3,500 trees so far.
- Both initiatives aim to reduce the urban heat island effect.
- Both initiatives are funded by the city's Environmental Sustainability Fund. Candidate Sentences: A) 'The Green Roofs Program provides grants for rooftop gardens on commercial buildings, and the Urban Forest Project plants trees along city streets.'
B) 'The Green Roofs Program, started in 2020, and the Urban Forest Project, started in 2021, are both funded by the city's Environmental Sustainability Fund and aim to reduce the urban heat island effect, though one covers 120 buildings and the other has planted 3,500 trees.'
C) 'Aiming to reduce the urban heat island effect and funded by the city's Environmental Sustainability Fund, the Green Roofs Program and the Urban Forest Project have covered 120 buildings with rooftop gardens and planted 3,500 trees, respectively.'
D) 'The Green Roofs Program and the Urban Forest Project differ in their methods—one creates rooftop gardens, the other plants street trees—but share the same funding source and the same goal of reducing the urban heat island effect.'

step_by_step.explanation: The prompt states: 'The student wants to emphasize what the two initiatives have in common.' The synthesis must foreground the shared features (goal, funding source) and may include the differences (methods, start dates, metrics) only if they are subordinated to the commonalities. The main clause should ideally contain the shared features, and the connectors should signal similarity or accumulation, not contrast.

Candidate A lists the two initiatives and their methods. It does not mention the shared features at all—the common goal and funding source are omitted. The sentence merely juxtaposes two facts without establishing any relationship between them. It fails to include the information most relevant to the goal, and it makes no move to emphasize commonality. Eliminate.

Candidate B includes the shared features ('both funded by... and aim to reduce...') in the main clause, placing the commonality in the position of structural emphasis. The differences (start dates, metrics) are included but subordinated in a concluding concessive clause ('though one covers... and the other has planted...'). The logical structure foregrounds commonality and backgrounds difference. This aligns well with the rhetorical goal. Keep.

Candidate C opens with the shared features in a participial phrase ('Aiming to reduce... and funded by...'), which subordinates them. The main clause is about the different methods and metrics, which are given the structural emphasis. The shared features, though mentioned, are backgrounded as contextual modifiers. The structure emphasizes differences, not commonalities. This misaligns with the rhetorical goal. Eliminate.

Candidate D opens with the word 'differ,' placing contrast in the position of emphasis. The shared features are mentioned but appear in a clause following 'but share,' which makes them feel like a concession to similarity within a contrast frame. The sentence's architecture is built around difference: 'differ in their methods... but share...' The rhetorical emphasis is on the contrast, not the commonality. This misaligns with the goal. Eliminate.

worked_examples.final_answer: Candidate B is the best synthesis. It places the shared features (funding source, goal) in the main clause, giving them structural emphasis, and subordinates the differences in a concluding concessive clause. The structure 'are both funded by... and aim to reduce...' foregrounds commonality, which is precisely what the rhetorical goal requires. Candidates C and D give structural emphasis to differences rather than similarities. Candidate A omits the shared features entirely.
worked_examples.concept_applied: This example demonstrates how main clause placement and logical connector choices determine whether a synthesized sentence emphasizes similarity or difference. The rhetorical goal dictates the structural design, and the correct answer is the one whose architecture aligns with that goal.

image.alt: Two versions of the same synthesized sentence with different emphases. In Version 1, shared features are in the main clause (highlighted) and differences are in a subordinate clause. In Version 2, differences are in the main clause and shared features are in a subordinate phrase. Arrows point to the emphasized information and label the rhetorical effect.
image.caption: Main clause placement is the primary tool of rhetorical emphasis: information in the main clause is foregrounded; subordinate information is backgrounded

sections.introduction: This section provides practice with the specific formats of SAT questions that require evaluating synthesized sentences. Students will work through prompts and notes with varying rhetorical goals and evaluate candidate sentences for structural alignment, information accuracy, and stylistic coherence.

key_concept.definition:
SAT note-combining questions follow a consistent format: a prompt states the rhetorical goal and provides bullet-point notes, followed by four candidate sentences. The correct sentence is the one that most effectively integrates the relevant information from the notes to achieve the stated goal, using appropriate structural tools—main clause placement, logical connectors, sequencing, and economy—to create a coherent and purposeful statement.

The strategic approach: (1) Identify the rhetorical goal and determine what structural design it requires—what should be in the main clause, what logical connectors are appropriate, what sequencing serves the purpose. (2) Note which bullet points are most relevant to the goal and which are secondary. (3) Evaluate each candidate against the structural requirements of the goal, eliminating choices whose architecture misaligns with the purpose. (4) For surviving choices, verify factual accuracy and stylistic coherence.
key_concept.context: Note-combining questions reward structural awareness. The student who analyzes how each candidate sentence is built—what is in the main clause, what connectors are used, what information comes first and last—will distinguish correct from incorrect choices efficiently.

categories.description: A prompt with a sequence goal and candidate sentences to evaluate.

examples.explanation:
Prompt: A student is writing a report about the discovery of a new species of frog. The student wants to trace the sequence of events that led to the discovery. Notes:
- 2019: Biologist Ana Reyes heard an unfamiliar frog call during a field survey in the cloud forests of Ecuador.
- 2020: Reyes returned to the site and collected several frog specimens.
- 2021: DNA analysis confirmed the frogs belonged to a previously unknown species.
- 2022: The new species was officially named and described in the journal Zootaxa.
- The frog, now named Pristimantis reyesae, is distinguished by its bright orange coloration and its unusually long toes.

Candidate Sentences: A) 'Pristimantis reyesae, a new species of frog distinguished by its bright orange coloration and unusually long toes, was officially named and described in the journal Zootaxa in 2022.'
B) 'After biologist Ana Reyes heard an unfamiliar frog call during a 2019 field survey in the cloud forests of Ecuador, she returned the following year to collect specimens, DNA analysis confirmed the frogs represented a new species in 2021, and the species was officially named Pristimantis reyesae in 2022.'
C) 'The discovery of the new frog species Pristimantis reyesae began in 2019 when biologist Ana Reyes heard an unfamiliar call in the Ecuadorian cloud forests and culminated in 2022 with the species' official naming in Zootaxa, following specimen collection in 2020 and confirmatory DNA analysis in 2021.'
D) 'Biologist Ana Reyes discovered a new species of frog in Ecuador, and it was named Pristimantis reyesae, and it has bright orange coloration and unusually long toes.'

Rhetorical goal: Trace the sequence of events. The synthesis must present the events in chronological or logical order, using appropriate sequencing language. The correct sentence should make the progression from initial discovery to final naming clear.

Candidate A: This sentence describes the frog but does not trace the sequence of discovery. It mentions only the final naming event and provides descriptive details about the frog's appearance. It fails to achieve the sequence goal. Eliminate.

Candidate B: This sentence presents the events in chronological order but uses choppy, unintegrated syntax—'she returned..., DNA analysis confirmed..., the species was officially named...' It reads as a list of independent clauses rather than a coherent sequenced narrative. The logic is temporal but the prose is stylistically weak. The lack of effective subordination and the run-on structure (comma splices) make this choice grammatically flawed as well. Eliminate.

Candidate C: This sentence presents the events in clear logical sequence, using appropriate sequencing language ('began in 2019,' 'culminated in 2022,' 'following...'). It integrates the descriptive detail (the frog's name) into the narrative of discovery. The sentence is structured around the temporal arc from beginning to end, with intermediate steps properly subordinated. This achieves the sequence goal with stylistic coherence. Keep.

Candidate D: This sentence lists facts in no particular order using only 'and' as a connector. It does not trace a sequence; it simply accumulates information. The descriptive detail about appearance is appended irrelevantly at the end. It fails to achieve the rhetorical goal. Eliminate.

Correct: Candidate C.

image.alt: An annotated practice question showing the prompt with the sequence goal highlighted, the notes with dates underscored, and candidate sentences with their structural features analyzed.
image.caption: SAT note-combining questions require evaluating how effectively a sentence's structure—its sequencing, subordination, and connectors—achieves the specified rhetorical goal

summary:
Combining notes into sentences is the fundamental skill of Rhetorical Synthesis. It requires the writer to take discrete pieces of information and integrate them into a single, coherent statement whose structural design—main clause placement, logical connectors, information sequencing, and stylistic economy—serves a specified rhetorical purpose. The same set of facts can be combined into many grammatically correct sentences, but only the sentence whose architecture aligns with the stated goal is correct on the SAT.

The structural tools of synthesis are the mechanisms through which a writer controls emphasis and relationship. The main clause carries the central assertion; what is placed there is foregrounded as the sentence's primary message. Subordinate clauses and phrases provide supporting information, backgrounded to varying degrees. Logical connectors explicitly signal the relationships among ideas—contrast, similarity, causation, sequence, concession, addition. The choice of connector determines how the reader understands the connection between facts. Information sequence determines what the reader encounters first and last, positions of natural emphasis that shape the sentence's rhetorical trajectory. Stylistic economy ensures that the sentence reads as a unified whole rather than a list of loosely connected fragments.

Incorrect answer choices on SAT note-combining questions fail in identifiable ways. Some place the wrong information in the main clause, emphasizing a detail tangential to the rhetorical goal while subordinating the information most relevant to the purpose. Some use logical connectors that misrepresent the relationships among the facts—using contrast language when similarity is needed, or causal language when only sequence is established. Some present information in a sequence that fails to serve the goal, or that is logically confused. Some are grammatically correct but stylistically weak, reading as choppy lists rather than smooth integrations. The correct choice is the one that deploys all the structural tools in service of the stated purpose, creating a sentence that is not only factually accurate and grammatically sound but rhetorically effective.
